cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ف ↔ He פ (p) — regular *p reflex; Ar final ه ↔ He ה (*h reflex); Arabic shows binary root reduction
cognates.notes: Arabic 'fam' (from *p-w-h) represents a noun form derived from the triliteral root, though it functions as a biliteral; Hebrew 'peh' shows the expected final *h preservation.

cognates.correspondence_path: Ar ف ↔ Syc ܦ (p); Ar م ↔ Syc ܡ (m). Note: The Aramaic root is traditionally treated as *p-w-m.
cognates.notes: The Aramaic/Syriac lexicalization (p-w-m) differs from the Arabic/Hebrew (p-h) path, reflecting a common Semitic divergence in naming the 'mouth' involving secondary developments.

caveats: v0 prototype: this is an LLM-generated analysis, not a lexicon lookup. Results may be incomplete or incorrect, especially for Syriac, Tigrinya, and rare forms. Verify against Wiktionary or a scholarly lexicon before citing.
The root p-h is one of the most debated in Semitic comparative studies due to the irregular behavior of the final weak consonant across daughter branches.
Arabic 'fam' is often analyzed as a reflex of an original *p-w-h, whereas Aramaic forms point toward a *p-w-m base.
